title: Renting a boat in Thailand: a practical guide to Phuket, Krabi and the islands
tags: boat rental, yacht charter, Thailand travel, sailing
topic: Renting a boat in Thailand: Phuket, Krabi and the islands
html:
There is a particular kind of disappointment that comes from visiting Thailand's Andaman coast and only seeing it from the back of a crowded ferry. The water is the colour of a swimming pool, the limestone karsts rise straight out of the sea, and yet you are queuing for a photo that two hundred other people are taking from the same spot. The simple fix is to get your own boat for a day or two. It is far less expensive than most first timers assume, and it changes the whole texture of a trip. If you have never done it before, here is what actually matters when you decide to rent a boat along this coast.

Why Phuket is the easiest place to start

Phuket is the obvious launch point because it has the most operators, the deepest range of boats and the best marinas. You can find everything from a basic long tail with a local skipper to a fully crewed catamaran with a chef on board. Most people sail out of Chalong, Ao Po or the Boat Lagoon area, and from there the famous spots are within easy reach. Phi Phi, Maya Bay, the Coral Islands and James Bond Island all sit within a comfortable day's cruising. The big advantage of going private is timing. You leave early, reach the headline lagoons before the ferries arrive, and you are already moving on by the time the day trippers turn up. If you want to compare what is on offer in one place rather than emailing ten separate companies, it is worth taking a few minutes to look through the listings and see the real range of options and prices side by side.

Krabi and the quieter side of the Andaman

Krabi has a different feel. The crowds thin out, the scenery somehow gets even better, and Railay, Hong Island and the Four Islands loop become the headline attractions. Departures from Ao Nang or Ao Thalane put you minutes from sheltered lagoons that you can have almost to yourself if you go midweek. A speedboat is the usual choice here because distances are short and the water inside the bays is calm. For couples and small groups, hiring a private speedboat for a day in Krabi is one of the better value experiences in the country. If you are weighing it up, the cost of a shared yacht charter split between four or six people often works out close to what you would pay for separate ferry tickets and entrance fees anyway, with none of the queuing.

Choosing between a long tail, a speedboat and a yacht

The boat you pick shapes the whole day, so it helps to be honest about what you want.

  • Long tail boats are cheap, characterful and perfect for short hops to a nearby beach. They are slow and open to the weather, so they suit relaxed half days rather than island marathons.
  • Speedboats cover ground quickly and let you string four or five islands together in a single day. They are the workhorse of the Andaman coast.
  • Catamarans and motor yachts are for people who want the journey to be the holiday: shade, a proper deck, snorkelling gear and somewhere comfortable to nap between swims.

Think about who is coming. A speedboat with young children for eight hours can be tiring, while a slow catamaran can feel sluggish for a group that wants to tick off a long list.

What to check before you book

A little homework saves a lot of frustration. When you find a boat you like, confirm a few practical points before you pay.

  1. Whether national park fees are included or paid separately on the day.
  2. Whether fuel, the skipper and snorkelling equipment are part of the quoted price.
  3. How weather cancellations are handled, since the Andaman can turn quickly in the green season.
  4. The exact pick up point, because Thai marinas are spread out and a wrong taxi can cost you an hour.

Reading recent reviews from other travellers tells you more than any glossy listing. A platform that lets you hire a boat with transparent pricing and verified operators removes most of the guesswork that used to come with arranging things on the beach the night before.

When to go and a note for boat owners

The high season on the Andaman coast runs roughly from November to April, when the sea is calmest and the visibility for snorkelling is at its best. May to October brings the monsoon, lower prices and emptier beaches, but you trade that for more cancelled days and choppier crossings. If you are flexible, the shoulder weeks in early November or late April can be a sweet spot.

One last thought. If you happen to own a boat in Thailand or anywhere else, the same marketplace that travellers use to book is also where you can put your boat to work during the months you are not using it.

Plenty of owners now list your boat alongside the charter fleets and earn through the busy season rather than leaving it idle at the dock. Whichever side of the deal you are on, getting onto the water is what turns a good Thailand trip into one you keep talking about.
